coredata.dc:title: Double emission CDs modified with rhodamine B for ratiometric sensing detection and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xiaoxiao
Zhang, Yan
Fu, Zheng
Tian, Jiashuo
Zhang, Liuping
Li, Wanqiong
Li, Shuailiang
Cui, Fengling
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, rhodamine B (RhB) was modified on carbon dots (CDs) by hydrothermal method, and a double emission ratiometric fluorescent probe (RhB-CDs) with red luminescence was successfully constructed. The synthesized RhB-CDs had exceptional optical properties and exhibit two emission peaks (480 nm and 590 nm) at 350 nm excitation. It could be used for ratiometric detection of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−, and the fluorescent color would also change (red to orange) when the pH changed from 6 to 7, showing great application potential in the field of sensing and detection. Moreover, the ratio of the emission peak fluorescence intensity had a linear relationship with the water content of alcohols, which was successfully used to detect actual water samples. In addition, the synthesized RhB-CDs had low cytotoxicity and were successfully used for multicolor cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red luminescent
Dual emission
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−
Selective and sensitive detection of RhB-CDs
Comparison of RhB and RhB-CDs UV images
Detection of water content in alcohols

Key Scientific Research Project of Henan Ministry of Education
National Natural Science Foundation of China-Union Foundation of Henan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Union Foundation of Henan ( U1704170 ), the Key Scientific Research Project of Henan Ministry of Education ( 20A610005 , 21A180015 ), the Key Programs for Science and Technology Development in Henan Province ( 192102310300 , 232102320219 ) and the doctor initiated project funding of Henan Normal University ( QD18083 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, D) TEM images and (B, E) particle size distribution of CDs and RhB-CDs (C,F) FTIR spectra of CDs and RhB-CDs.
(A) Full spectrum and (BD) High resolution spectra of CDs (E) Full spectrum and (FI) High resolution spectra of RhB-CDs.

(A) Emission spectra at different excitations (B) UV absorption of CDs, RhB and RhB-CDs (inset: Irradiation of RhB-CDs solution at 365 nm UV light) (C) The quantum yield diagram of CDs and (D) The quantum yield diagram of RhB-CDs (E) The fluorescence lifetime diagram of CDs and RhB-CDs (F) Comparison of emission peaks of CDs and RhB-CDs.

(A) Emission peak intensity changes of RhB-CDs after the addition of MnO4 − (B) Linear relationship between the MnO4 − concentration and fluorescence intensity ratio F480/F590 (C) Emission peak intensity changes of RhB-CDs after the addition of Cr2O7 2− (D) Linear relationship between the Cr2O7 2− concentration and fluorescence intensity ratio F480/F590.
(A) UV absorption peak changes of RhB and (B) RhB-CDs after the addition of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−.

Fluorescence intensity changes of (A) RhB and (B) RhB-CDs at pH 3–10 (C) The fluorescence intensity of RhB-CDs changed at pH 6–7 (Illustration: Linear diagram at pH 6.4–6.7) (D) The UV-light illumination diagram of RhB-CDs in the pH 3–10.

(A) The emission peak change of RhB-CDs under different water content (B) The linear relationship between the water content and the fluorescence intensity ratio of F480/F590 (C) Water content test in methanol (D) Water content test in isopropyl alcohol.
Fluorescence lifetime variation diagram The Stern Volmer diagram of fluorescence quenching of RhB-CDs by (B) MnO4 − and (C) Cr2O7 2− (D) The UV–visible absorption spectra of MnO4 −and Cr2O7 2−.
(A) Cell viability at different concentrations of RhB-CDs Confocal images of (B) Channel 1, λem = 460–560 nm (C) Channel 2, λem = 560–660 nm (D) overlap image.

head.ce:title: Double emission CDs modified with rhodamine B for ratiometric sensing detection and bioimaging

Henan University of Animal Husbandry and Economy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Henan Institute of Technology, Xinxiang 453000, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, rhodamine B (RhB) was modified on carbon dots (CDs) by hydrothermal method, and a double emission ratiometric fluorescent probe (RhB-CDs) with red luminescence was successfully constructed. The synthesized RhB-CDs had exceptional optical properties and exhibit two emission peaks (480 nm and 590 nm) at 350 nm excitation. It could be used for ratiometric detection of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−, and the fluorescent color would also change (red to orange) when the pH changed from 6 to 7, showing great application potential in the field of sensing and detection. Moreover, the ratio of the emission peak fluorescence intensity had a linear relationship with the water content of alcohols, which was successfully used to detect actual water samples. In addition, the synthesized RhB-CDs had low cytotoxicity and were successfully used for multicolor cell imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a kind of nanomaterials with superior optical properties, have attracted researchers' attention due to their extensive applications in the fields of biological imaging [1–4], sensor detection [5–7], drug delivery [8–11], etc. In recent years, in order to reduce the interference of background fluorescence and damage to biological tissues, researchers began to focus on the synthesis of red emission carbon dots and made great progress. For example, Zhang et al. [12] successfully extracted red emission carbon dots from citric acid and urea in formic acid by solvothermal method. The quantum yield in dimethyl sulfoxide solution was as high as 43.4 %, without cumbersome purification steps. Wang et al. [13] synthesized the red emission carbon dots by solvothermal method using 1,1′-binaphthyl-2,2′-diamine and citric acid as carbon sources. It could be used to detect Fe3+ and F−, and also had good applications in solid state luminescence. Cao et al. [14] successfully synthesized red luminescent N,Cl co-doped CDs for the detection of tea polyphenols, with R2 of 0.999 in the concentration range of 1–20 μg/mL. In addition, the N,Cl co-doped CDs could also be used for lysosomal targeting, and the Pearson coefficient was up to 0.991.

However, CDs with single emission peak are easily interfered by the environment and the instrument itself during detection, resulting in data deviation. Ratiometric fluorescent CDs can avoid the above drawbacks through intrinsic self-calibration [15,16]. Therefore, some achievements have been made by many researchers in the design and construction of ratiometric fluorescent CDs. For example, Farid Ahmed's group [17] has synthesized europium-doped dual-emission CDs with two emission peaks (445 and 617 nm) at a single excitation wavelength of 275 nm. After the addition of chloramphenicol (CAP), Hg2+ and Fe3+, the two emission peaks have different changes respectively, so as to achieve the purpose of detection. Zou et al. [18] synthesize carbon dots with bromine substitution sites in one step by hydrothermal method, and then grafted different reaction groups on CDs. That is, a ratiometric fluorescent probe is constructed by combining two substances with different emission wavelengths, which has been successfully used for the detection of HCHO, HSO3 − and biological imaging. Bogireddy et al. [19] have synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (DECDs) with dual emission peaks by microwave method using citric acid and urea as carbon source. By changing the reaction conditions, the emission peak can be modulated from 430 nm to 512 nm. And it has good stability and selectivity to acetone, Cr(VI) and Fe(III).

In this paper, CDs was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid as carbon source. Subsequently, rhodamine B was modified on CDs by hydrothermal method to successfully synthesize red luminescent CDs (RhB-CDs) with dual emission peaks. The RhB-CDs had obvious emission signal at 480 nm and 590 nm when excited at 350 nm. It could be used for ratiometric fluorescence detection of MnO4 2− and Cr2O7 2−, as well as the detection of water content in ethanol. It was worth noting that RhB-CDs were also successfully applied to multicolor cell imaging by taking advantage of their double emission properties. Under the excitation of 405 nm laser, A549 cells incubated with RhB-CDs could simultaneously take fluorescence images in the red channel and the green channel.

The reagents used during the experiments such as o-phenylenediamine (Sahn Chemical Technology Co., LTD.), ascorbic acid (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD.), Rhodamine (Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., LTD.) and Trimethyl (Aladdin Group Co., LTD.) were analytical grade and the water was high purity water.

The infrared spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet FTS NEXUS) was used to test the FT-IR spectra. XPS spectra data were obtained by X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (ESCALAB250Xi, USA). The fluorescence spectra were scanned by a Perkinelmer (LS-55) spectrophotometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained by UV–Vis spectrometer (Agilent technology, Cary100). Absolute quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime data were obtained using the fluorescence spectrometer (FLS980, Edinburgh).

First, 0.2 g o-phenylenediamine and 0.2 g ascorbic acid were dissolved in 15 mL H2O by sonication for 10 min. Then, it was placed in a high-pressure reactor and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction was completed, it was naturally cooled to room temperature. Next, the obtained yellow solution was centrifuged (12,000 rpm), the supernatant was filtered (0.22 μm filter membrane). Eventually, the obtained CDs were stored at 4 °C for the next step.

0.2 g rhodamine B was added to 15 mL of synthesized CDs solution (15 mg/mL), and then ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min. The mixture was placed in a high pressure reactor at 140 °C for 13 h, and the red solution of RhB-CDs was obtained. Finally, the synthesized RhB-CDs were centrifuged, filtered, and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2− to RhB-CDs were tested. Different concentrations (0–100 μM) of MnO4 − or Cr2O7 2− were added to 100 μL RhB-CDs solution. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of RhB-CDs was tested and recorded on the fluorescence spectrometer. The concentration of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2− had a great influence on the intensity of the two emission peaks of RhB-CDs with good linear relationship.

The absolute quantum yield of CDs was measured using the integrating sphere on the FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer without the need for a standard as a control. The excitation wavelength was selected at 350 nm, and the emission of CDs solution and solvent was scanned and integrated respectively. The absolute quantum yield of CDs was calculated using the following formula:
Φ a
= ε / α = ∫
L emission
/
∫
E solvent
/ ∫
E sample
where Φa was absolute quantum yield, ε was the photon emitted by the sample, α represented photons absorbed by the sample, Lemission was the emission spectrum of the sample, Esample and Esolvent represented the excitation spectrum of the sample and excitation spectra of solvents respectively.

Tris-HCl buffer solution was selected to study the influence of different pH conditions on RhB-CDs solution. The intensity and fluorescence color of RhB-CDs were observed by mixing hydrochloric acid buffer solution of different pH values with RhB-CDs solution at 9:1 volume ratio.

A water-ethanol mixture with different water content was prepared using high purity water and absolute ethanol. 100 μL of RhB-CDs solution was added to 900 μL of mixture with different water content, and fluorescence intensity changes were recorded on a fluorescence spectrometer.

The cytotoxicity of RhB-CDs to A549 cells was determined by CCK-8 method and fluorescence images were taken. First, 100 μL A549 cells, with a cell density of approximately 5000 cells/well, were incubated in 96-wells plates for 24 h. The supernatant in the 96-wells plates was then replaced with different concentrations of 100 μL RhB-CDs solution, and the incubation was continued for 24 h. Finally, the CCK-8 solution (CCK-8 to medium volume ratio of 1:9) was prepared and added 100 μL to each well. After incubation for 1 h, absorbance was measured on the microplate.

The A549 cells (35,000 cells/mL) were incubated in confocal dishes for 24 h, and then RhB-CDs were added for further incubation for 24 h. Whereafter, a 405 nm laser was selected to take fluorescence images in channel 1 (460–560 nm) and channel 2 (560–660 nm) using a confocal laser scanning microscope.

The images of CDs and RhB-CDs were taken by transmission electron microscope to understand and compare their morphology and particle size. Fig. 1A and B exhibits the TEM images and particle size distribution of CDs respectively. The images showed that the CDs are spherical particles with good dispersion. Then 100 random particles were analyzed to calculate the particle size. The data results indicated that the particle size distribution of the synthesized CDs was between 1.67 nm and 3.21 nm, with an average particle size of 2.39 nm. Fig. 1D and E displays the morphology and particle size distribution of RhB-CDs. The particle size distribution of RhB-CDs was relatively wide, ranging from 5.16 nm to 9.82 nm. The average particle size was 7.49 nm through calculation. Compared with CDs, the particle size of RhB-CDs was significantly increased, and the particle size distribution was wider, which may be due to the successful modification of rhodamine B on CDs.

In order to distinguish the specific structure and element compositions of CDs and RhB-CDs, their infrared and XPS spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 1C and F, the surface functional groups of CDs and RhB-CDs were compared by infrared spectroscopy. In the infrared spectrum of RhB-CDs, the absorption peaks at 3218.47 cm−1 and 2930.22 cm−1 were due to the presence of O-H/N-H/C-H [20,21]. The peak at 1686.49 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibration of CO and CC [22]. Moreover, the peaks at 1424.07 cm−1 and 1020.06 cm−1 correspond to the CN and CO stretching vibration [23,24]. The position of the absorption peaks of CDs was roughly the same as that of RhB-CDs, but the peak intensity was slightly different. This indicated that the surface functional groups of RhB-CDs and CDs were basically the same, but the content had changed. This might be because that part of the double bond was broken during synthesis.

Fig. 2A-D represent the XPS spectra of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, three peaks could be seen at 284.8, 399.1 and 531.7 eV, indicating that CDs were composed of C (64.08 %), N(5.72 %) and O(30.2 %), respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2B) exhibited four peaks at 284.61, 286.01, 287.47 and 288.43 eV, revealing the presence of C=C/C-C, CO and CO bonds on the surface of CDs [25–27]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) displayed three peaks at 400.1 eV, 401.5 eV and 406.4 eV, assigned to amino N, pyrrolic N and NH, respectively [28,29]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) could be deconvoluted into two peaks corresponding to CO at the 531.92 eV and CO at 532.65 eV, respectively [30,31]. Meanwhile, the XPS spectra of RhB-CDs (Fig. 2E-I) were also tested for comparison. The content of C, N and O elements in RhB-CDs was 62.11 %, 6.13 % and 30.91 %, respectively, and the content of Cl element was 0.85 % (Fig. 2E). Three distinct peaks in the C1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2F) indicated the existence of C=C/C-C (284.53 eV), C-O/C-N (285.96 eV), CO (288.57 eV) [32,33]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2G) confirmed the presence of pyridinic N (399.35 eV), amino N (400.47 eV), pyrrolic N (401.35 eV) and NH (406.69 eV)28, 29. In O1s spectrum (Fig. 2H), the appearance of 531.64 eV and 532.48 eV peaks was due to the existence of CO and CO [34]. Fig. 2I displays the Cl2p high-resolution spectrum. It was found that two distinct peaks at 197.51 eV and 199.05 eV, indicating the presence of CCl and COO-Cl [35]. Compared with CDs, the N and Cl elements of RhB-CDs were increased, which was because rhodamine B contained pyridine N group and Cl elements. Therefore, the results could further confirm the successful modification of Rhodamine B.

The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra was scanned to explore the optical properties of RhB-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3A, the RhB-CDs displayed double emission at 480 nm and 590 nm. Fig. 3B shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, RhB and RhB-CDs. It could be seen that RhB had a clear absorption peak at about 550 nm, CDs had an absorption peak at about 280 nm, and RhB-CDs had obvious absorption peaks at about 550 nm and 280 nm. Compared with the absorption peaks of CDs, it was easy to see that after modifying RhB on CDs, a clear absorption peak of RhB appeared at about 550 nm, indicating the successful synthesis of RhB-CDs. The inset of Fig. 3B presented the picture of RhB-CDs under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). Under natural light, the synthesized RhB-CDs appeared as a reddish-brown liquid and emitted bright red light under 365 nm UV light. The excellent optical properties of the CDs laid a good foundation for future applications. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3C and D, Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3C and D, the quantum yields of CDs and RhB-CDs were 26.85 % and 59.68 %, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime values were 9.7002 ns and 8.6470 ns, respectively. The excellent optical properties of the synthesized RhB-CDs were also reflected in their ultra-high quantum yield and long fluorescence lifetime, which were superior to most CDs. Fig. 3F shows the comparison of emission wavelengths of CDs and RhB-CDs. The above data indicated that the properties of CDs changed significantly after the addition of RhB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of RhB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The selective detection of RhB-CDs for other ions and amino acids was studied, and the specific results are shown in the Fig. 4A and B. It could be clearly seen from the figure that only after adding MnO4 −, the fluorescence intensity ratio of RhB-CDs to F480/F590 was significantly different from that after adding other ions. The reason why F480/F590 value did not changed after adding Cr2O7 2− was that the emission peak intensity at 480 nm and 590 nm decreased by the same amplitude. (Numbers 1–28 in Fig. 4A represent: Ni2+, Cu2+, Sn4+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, K+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Co2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, Na+, Mg2+, SO4 −, BrO3 −, I−, P2O7 4−, IO4 −, CrO4 −, Br−, ClO3 −, Cr2O7 2−, Cr2+, Cr3+, MnO4 −. Numbers 1–14 in Fig. 4B represent: Val, Ser, Glu, Lys, Asp, Ile, AA, Leu, Cys-cys, Cys, Tyr, Met, GSH, Hcy).

During the study, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of the two emission peaks of RhB-CDs would change significantly after adding MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−. When the concentration of MnO4 − gradually increased (0–100 μM), the fluorescence intensity of emission peaks at 480 nm gradually increased and decreased at 590 nm (Fig. 5A). Fig. 5B showed the linear relationship between the intensity ratio F480/F590 and the concentration of MnO4 −. With the increase of MnO4 − concentration, the ratio of F480/F590 gradually increased and showed a linear relationship, with the R2 value of 0.9942. As pictured in Fig. 5C, the emission intensity at 480 nm and 590 nm gradually decreased with the increase of the Cr2O7 2− concentration (0–300 μM) under 350 nm excitation. As could be seen from Fig. 5D, when the concentration of Cr2O7 2− increased, the fluorescence intensity ratio F480/F590 showed a downward trend, and its R2 value was 0.9996. The results confirmed that the RhB-CDs could be used for ratiometric fluorescence detection of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of RhB and RhB-CDs UV images
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6A shows the change of UV absorption peaks after adding MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2− to RhB. It could be seen that the peak position of RhB at 550 nm did not change significantly, only the absorption peak intensity before 450 nm changed. Fig. 6B shows that the absorption peak at 550 nm disappeared after the addition of MnO4 − to RhB-CDs, a new peak appeared at about 500 nm, and the intensity of the absorption peak at 300 nm was significantly increased. After the addition of Cr2O7 2−, the absorption peak at about 550 nm did not change significantly. However, a new absorption peak appeared at 350 nm, and the intensity and position of the absorption peak at other positions also changed.

The change of fluorescence intensity of RhB at 590 nm was tested under different pH conditions. As shown in the Fig. 7A, the fluorescence remained essentially unchanged at pH 3–10. Surprisingly, the fluorescence intensity and luminescence of RhB-CDs was changed significantly under different pH conditions. Fig. 7B displays the intensity ratio F480/F590 changes of RhB-CDs with pH from 3 to 10. In particular, when pH changed from 6 to 7, the fluorescence intensity was significantly quenched. Meanwhile, we further tested the fluorescence intensity changes in the range of pH 6–7, and found that the fluorescence intensity changed significantly in pH 6.4–6.7, and had a good linear relationship, with a R2 value of 0.9946 (Fig. 7C). The change in pH from 3 to 10 was accompanied by a change in fluorescence color from red to orange (Fig. 7D). The results suggested that the RhB-CDs could roughly identify the pH environment by the naked eye, and it had a good application prospect in pH detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in alcohols
ce:section.ce:para: It was found that the fluorescence intensity of RhB-CDs decreased significantly after the addition of ethanol, so we applied it to the detection of water content in ethanol. As shown in Fig. 8A, when the water content increased from 0 % to 100 %, the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm gradually increased and that at 590 nm gradually decreased. The results demonstrated that RhB-CDs could also be used for the determination of water content by ratiometric fluorescence. Fig. 8B exhibits the linear relationship between the ratio of water content and fluorescence intensity with R2 value of 0.994. Meanwhile, the RhB-CDs can also be used to detect water content in methanol and isopropyl alcohol. As shown in Fig. 8C and D, when the water content of methanol and isopropyl alcohol ranged from 10 % to 50 % and from 10 % to 90 % respectively, the fluorescence intensity had a good linear relationship with water content. The results indicated that RhB-CDs were a kind of good material for water content detection.

The fluorescence lifetime of RhB-CDs was tested by adding MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2− (Fig. 9 ). The calculation results showed that the fluorescence lifetime of RhB-CDs was 8.5834 ns. After adding MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−, the fluorescence lifetime changed to 8.8219 ns and 8.0317 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime had not changed significantly. It was speculated that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching might be static quenching. Meanwhile, the UV absorption peak was further determined, and the peaks changed significantly after the addition of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2− to RhB-CDs (Fig. 6B). This might be due to the generation of ground-state complexes, further demonstrating the occurrence of static quenching. We used Stern-Volmer equation: F0 / F = 1 + KSV [Q] to further validate the possible cause of fluorescence quenching, F0 represents the ratio of fluorescence intensity F480/F590 in the absence of quencher, F is the ratio of fluorescence intensity F480/F590 after adding MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−, [Q] is the concentration of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−. As shown in Fig. 9B and C, F0/F showed a good linear relationship with MnO4 −and Cr2O7 2−. The Ksv values were 3.8 × 103 L mol−1 and 3.0 × 102 L mol−1, respectively, and the calculated Kq values were 4.427 × 1011 L mol−1 and 3.495 × 1010 L mol−1, respectively. This is greater than the maximum possible value of dynamic quenching (2 × 1010 L mol−1 S−1). In addition, we tested the ultraviolet absorption peaks of the quenching agents MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2− (Fig. 9D), and found that MnO4 − had absorption peaks at 250–600 nm, which overlapped with the excitation and emission of RhB-CDs. However, because the quenching absorption peak was mainly at 590 nm, there was no overlap. Therefore, we speculated that the fluorescence quenching might be caused by the internal filtration effect. Cr2O7 2− has an absorption peak near 250 nm and 350 nm, which overlaps with the excitation peak of RhB-CDs, which may be due to the internal filtration effect.

The water content was tested using synthetic RhB-CDs with medical alcohol as an actual sample. As shown in Table 1 , in 75 % alcohol, the detected water content was 25.61 % and the relative error was 2.44 %, indicating that the synthesized RhB-CDs could be used to detect water content.

In order to further applied RhB-CDs to organism, the toxicity of RhB-CDs was first assessed using A549 cells, and then confocal imaging was performed. Fig. 10A reflected the cell viability of A549 cells after treatment with different concentrations of RhB-CDs. The results showed that even if the concentration of RhB-CDs reached 0.25 mg/mL, the viability of A549 cells was higher than 80 %. It demonstrated that the RhB-CDs synthesized in this study had little toxic and appropriate for biological imaging. Therefore, RhB-CDs were used to incubate A549 cells in confocal dishes. Fig. 10B and C display the confocal images taken in the green channel (460–560 nm) and the red channel (560–660 nm) under the excitation of 405 nm laser, respectively. Fig. 10D exhibits the overlap of the two channels. After incubated with RhB-CDs, A549 cells showed different fluorescent colors under 405 nm excitation, which indicated that the synthesized RhB-CDs could enter the cells and was a promising imaging material.

In summary, the green luminescent carbon dots were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid as carbon sources, and then rhodamine B was modified on CDs to obtain red luminescent double emission CDs (RhB-CDs). After the addition of MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−, the fluorescence intensity of emission peaks at 480 nm and 590 nm would be quenched to different degrees. According to the change of intensity ratio F480/F590, it could be used to detect MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−. In addition, when the pH changed from 6 to 7, the fluorescence intensity would be greatly weakened, and the luminescence color would change from red to orange. The general pH environment of RhB-CDs could be judged by the naked eye. The RhB-CDs could also be used to detect water content. When RhB-CDs were in different water content environment, the two emission summits changed differently, and the ratio of water content had a good linear relationship with fluorescence intensity, which had been successfully used to detect water content in real samples. Moreover, by evaluating the toxicity and imaging performance of the material, it was found that the synthesized RhB-CDs had low toxicity and could be used for multicolor cell imaging, which was a kind of superior sensing and imaging material with good application prospect.

Xiaoxiao Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yan Zhang: Biological experiment Supervisor, Visualization. Zheng Fu: Software, Visualization. Jiashuo Tian and Liuping Zhang: Data curation. Wanqiong Li and Shuailiang Li: Validation. Fengling Cui: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Union Foundation of Henan (U1704170), the Key Scientific Research Project of Henan Ministry of Education (20A610005, 21A180015), the Key Programs for Science and Technology Development in Henan Province (192102310300, 232102320219) and the doctor initiated project funding of Henan Normal University (QD18083).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-doped and non-modified carbon dots with high quantum yield for the chemosensing of uric acid and living cell imaging
Anal. Chim. Acta
